With an automatic verifier this amplifies weak models past stronger single-shot ones (DeepSeek-Coder-V2 reaches 56% on SWE-bench Lite vs the 43% single-attempt SOTA), but on unverifiable math, majority-vote and reward-model selection plateau near 41% while coverage climbs to 98.44%—exposing verification, not generation, as the bottleneck.

papers.title: Understanding R1-Zero-Like Training: A Critical Perspective
papers.authors: Liu, Chen, Li, Qi, Pang, Du, Lee, Lin
papers.abstract: A critical study of R1-Zero training that separates the base model from the RL. Shows Qwen2.5 base models are already near-SFT math solvers (best with no template, a ~60% jump) and that GRPO's length normalizer mechanically inflates incorrect responses; introduces Dr. GRPO, a one-line unbiased fix that recovers a PPO/RLOO gradient and improves token efficiency. The minimalist recipe (Qwen2.5-Math-7B + Dr. GRPO) reaches 43.3% on AIME 2024 in 27 GPU-hours.

papers.title: SimpleRL-Zoo: Investigating and Taming Zero Reinforcement Learning for Open Base Models in the Wild
papers.authors: Zeng, Huang, Liu, Liu, He, Ma, He
papers.abstract: A systematic study of DeepSeek-R1-style "zero RL training"—GRPO straight from a base model with a pure correctness reward—across 10 open base models including Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B/24B, DeepSeek-Math-7B, and the full Qwen2.5 family. It shows the recipe lifts every model (e.g. Mistral-Small-24B's six-benchmark math average 27.6→49.6), that the reflective "aha moment" emerges from scratch in small non-Qwen models while Qwen2.5 already had it, and that response length alone is a misleading signal—format rewards, mismatched data difficulty, and SFT cold-starts each sabotage training.

papers.title: On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?
papers.abstract: The foundational critique of the scale-is-progress paradigm: ever-larger language models buy fluency, not understanding—a "stochastic parrot" stitches together linguistic form by probability with no reference to meaning—while their environmental, financial, and bias costs go undocumented and fall hardest on the communities least likely to benefit. Coined the term and anchored the harms-side counterweight to the scaling narrative.

papers.title: On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models
papers.abstract: The 200-plus-page Stanford report (the founding document of CRFM) that coined the term "foundation models": models trained on broad data at scale and adaptable to a wide range of downstream tasks. It frames the paradigm around two forces—emergence (capabilities like in-context learning appear with scale, unprogrammed) and homogenization (one base model underlies everything, making it a single point of failure)—then surveys the capabilities, underlying technology, applications, and societal risks of the paradigm.

papers.title: Multitask Prompted Training Enables Zero-Shot Task Generalization
papers.abstract: Recasts 62 supervised NLP datasets into 2,073 diverse natural-language prompts (the Public Pool of Prompts, P3) and fine-tunes an 11B encoder-decoder (T5+LM) on a 39-dataset multitask mixture, holding out four whole task types. The resulting model, T0, matches or beats GPT-3 175B on 9 of 11 held-out datasets at ~16× smaller scale — showing zero-shot task generalization can be induced by explicit multitask prompted training, and that training on more diverse prompts improves robustness to wording.

papers.title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
papers.authors: Park, O'Brien, Cai, Morris, Liang, Bernstein
papers.abstract: Introduces a memory-stream architecture that extends an LLM (gpt-3.5-turbo) into believable agents: an append-only natural-language log of experience, retrieved by recency × importance × relevance, recursively synthesized into reflections, and decomposed into top-down plans. Twenty-five agents in a Sims-style sandbox (Smallville) produce emergent social behavior—information diffusion (party awareness 4%→52%), relationship formation (network density 0.167→0.74), and coordination (a self-organized Valentine's Day party)—and an ablation shows observation, planning, and reflection each contribute to believability.

papers.title: Do As I Can, Not As I Say: Grounding Language in Robotic Affordances
papers.authors: Ahn, Brohan, Ichter, Levine, Xia, Zeng, …
papers.abstract: Grounds a frozen LLM planner in a robot's physical capabilities by multiplying the LLM's score for each candidate skill ("Say") by a learned affordance value function for that skill ("Can"), so the chosen plan is both useful and feasible. PaLM-SayCan completes 84% of plans and 74% of executions across 101 long-horizon kitchen instructions on a real mobile manipulator, and improves directly as the underlying language model improves.

papers.title: Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models
papers.authors: Wang, Xie, Jiang, Mandlekar, Xiao, Zhu, Fan, Anandkumar
papers.abstract: The first LLM-powered embodied lifelong-learning agent in Minecraft. A frozen GPT-4, queried as a black box (no fine-tuning), drives three interacting loops: an automatic curriculum that proposes its own goals from the agent's live state, an ever-growing skill library of executable Mineflayer-JS code retrieved by embedding similarity, and an iterative prompting mechanism that refines code from environment feedback, execution errors, and GPT-4 self-verification. It obtains 3.3x more unique items (63 in 160 iterations), travels 2.3x farther, unlocks tech-tree tiers up to 15.3x faster than prior agents, is the only method to reach the diamond tool (1 of 3 runs), and zero-shot-transfers its skill library to solve unseen tasks in a fresh world.

papers.title: τ-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
papers.abstract: A customer-service benchmark where an LLM agent must gather information from a simulated human user, follow a written domain-policy document, and reproduce its own success across repeated trials. Introduces the pass^k reliability metric alongside two synthetic domains (τ-retail, τ-airline); even gpt-4o's function-calling agent averages only 48.2% success and its pass^8 reliability falls below 25%.

papers.title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents
papers.abstract: Four fully-hostable, real-world-scale websites (e-commerce, forum, GitLab, CMS) with 812 long-horizon tasks scored by functional correctness rather than reference-trajectory matching. Even GPT-4 with chain-of-thought reasoning closes only 14.41% of tasks, against a 78.24% human baseline.

papers.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants
papers.authors: Mialon, Fourrier, Swift, Wolf, LeCun, Scialom
papers.abstract: 466 human-crafted, human-validated questions that are conceptually trivial for people (92% human accuracy) but require an AI assistant to autonomously reason, browse the web, read diverse file types, and use tools to reach a single unambiguous factual answer — GPT-4 with plugins manages only about 15% overall and 0% on the hardest tier.

papers.title: RewardBench: Evaluating Reward Models for Language Modeling
papers.abstract: A common benchmark and evaluation codebase for reward models — the classifiers and DPO checkpoints that decide what counts as a better answer during RLHF — spanning chat, reasoning, safety, and legacy preference data across 80+ open models, exposing a wide, previously invisible gap between classifier-trained and DPO-derived reward models.

papers.title: JudgeBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating LLM-based Judges
papers.abstract: Builds JudgeBench, a 350-pair benchmark of objectively correct-vs-incorrect response pairs across knowledge, reasoning, math, and coding, sampled from a single strong model so pairs are style-matched, then labeled via each source dataset's own verifier. Vanilla-prompted GPT-4o scores just 50.86% (near-random) at telling right from wrong; only reasoning-heavy models or specialized reward models meaningfully beat chance.

papers.title: FrontierMath: A Benchmark for Evaluating Advanced Mathematical Reasoning in AI
papers.authors: Glazer, Erdil, Besiroglu, Chicharro, Chen, …, Moulinos, Ducey
papers.abstract: Hundreds of original, unpublished mathematics problems written and vetted by 60+ research mathematicians, checked by automated verification rather than human proof-grading — as of publication, every frontier LLM solved fewer than 2% of them.

papers.title: Program Synthesis with Large Language Models
papers.abstract: MBPP is a crowd-sourced benchmark of 974 short, one-sentence Python problems (each with a reference solution and three test asserts) isolating natural-language-to-code synthesis from the narrative competitive-programming style of contemporaries like APPS and Codex/HumanEval; few-shot synthesis scales log-linearly with model size, and four turns of one-sentence human feedback raise the hard-problem solve rate from 30% to over 65%.

papers.title: RE-Bench: Evaluating Frontier AI R&D Capabilities of Language Model Agents against Human Experts
papers.authors: Wijk, Lin, Becker, …, Saunders, West, Barnes
papers.abstract: METR's benchmark pitting frontier AI agents against 61 real ML experts (71 runs) on 7 hand-crafted AI-research-engineering tasks under matched 8-hour budgets. Agents score roughly 4x higher than humans at a 2-hour budget, but humans keep improving while agent scores plateau, reaching about 2x the best agent's score at 32 hours — despite agents running at roughly 1/15th the cost per attempt.

papers.title: AI Agents That Matter
papers.abstract: A methodology critique of AI agent evaluation: three trivial baselines (retry, escalation, temperature warming) match or beat three published complex agent architectures on HumanEval at up to 55x less cost, alongside diagnoses of conflated model/downstream evaluation, missing benchmark holdouts, ignored human-in-the-loop supervision, and widespread reproducibility bugs.

papers.title: BetterBench: Assessing AI Benchmarks, Uncovering Issues, and Establishing Best Practices
papers.authors: Reuel, A. Hardy, Smith, Lamparth, M. Hardy, Kochenderfer
papers.abstract: A 46-criteria assessment framework audits 24 widely-used AI benchmarks across their design, implementation, documentation, and maintenance lifecycle, finding large quality gaps — e.g. MMLU scores lowest of all 24 while GPQA scores among the highest — and that most benchmarks lack replication scripts or statistical-significance reporting.

papers.title: The Leaderboard Illusion
papers.abstract: An audit of 2M Chatbot Arena battles across 243 models and 42 providers finds undisclosed private testing (Meta tested 27 variants before Llama 4), asymmetric data access (Google and OpenAI received an estimated 19.2% and 20.4% of all Arena data), and silent model deprecation (205 of 243 models) that systematically favor a handful of proprietary providers over open-weight and open-source ones.

papers.title: Adding Error Bars to Evals: A Statistical Approach to Language Model Evaluations
papers.abstract: A statistical framework that treats eval questions as draws from an unseen super-population, giving closed-form standard errors (plain and clustered), variance-reduction techniques, paired significance tests between models, and a sample-size/power-analysis formula for planning eval size — used to show that Llama 3's published confidence intervals were too narrow on some evals and too wide on others.

papers.title: LiveBench: A Challenging, Contamination-Limited LLM Benchmark
papers.abstract: A monthly-refreshed, 18-task LLM benchmark spanning math, coding, reasoning, data analysis, instruction following, and language comprehension, scored entirely by objective ground truth rather than an LLM judge; even the top model at release, o1-preview, clears only 64.7%.

papers.title: ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-world APIs
papers.abstract: An automated ChatGPT pipeline builds ToolBench — 126,486 instruction-and-solution-path traces over 16,464 real RapidAPI endpoints — plus a depth-first search decision tree (DFSDT) that finds working multi-tool solutions where ReAct gets stuck; fine-tuning LLaMA-2 7B on it produces ToolLLaMA, which lands within a few points of ChatGPT on the paper's own ToolEval suite and generalizes to unseen APIs.

papers.title: VisualWebArena: Evaluating Multimodal Agents on Realistic Visual Web Tasks
papers.abstract: A 910-task benchmark extending WebArena with genuinely visually-grounded web tasks across Classifieds, Shopping, and Reddit sites; the best VLM agent (GPT-4V + Set-of-Marks) reaches 16.4% success against an 88.7% human ceiling.

papers.title: Evaluating Very Long-Term Conversational Memory of LLM Agents
papers.authors: Maharana, Lee, Tulyakov, Bansal, Barbieri, Fang
papers.abstract: Introduces LoCoMo, 50 synthetic-but-human-edited very-long-term conversations (avg. 304.9 turns, 19.3 sessions, 9,209 tokens, spanning months) generated by persona- and temporal-event-graph-conditioned LLM agents, plus a three-task benchmark (QA, event summarization, multimodal dialogue generation) on which the best model tested, GPT-4-turbo, reaches only 32.1 F1 against a human score of 87.9.

papers.title: Beyond Goldfish Memory: Long-Term Open-Domain Conversation
papers.abstract: Introduces Multi-Session Chat (MSC), a crowdsourced dataset of human-human conversations spread across up to 5 sessions (hours-to-days apart) with crowdworker-written summaries linking sessions, plus retrieval- and summarization-memory-augmented models that outperform standard encoder-decoder Transformers on both perplexity and human evaluation for this long-term, multi-session setting.

papers.title: Asynchronous Methods for Deep Reinforcement Learning
papers.authors: Mnih, Badia, Mirza, Graves, Lillicrap, Harley, Silver, Kavukcuoglu
papers.abstract: Introduces asynchronous variants of four RL algorithms — one-step Sarsa, one-step Q-learning, n-step Q-learning, and advantage actor-critic — where multiple actor-learner threads exploring their own copy of the environment update one shared network via lock-free Hogwild!-style gradients, replacing DQN's experience-replay buffer as the stabilizer. The best variant, A3C, trains on just 16 CPU cores (no GPU) and beats DQN-family state of the art on 57 Atari games in about half the wall-clock training time, while also being the first of the four to work on continuous-control MuJoCo tasks and a novel 3D maze-navigation domain.

papers.title: Trust Region Policy Optimization
papers.abstract: Proves a monotonic-improvement guarantee for general stochastic policies and turns it into a practical algorithm: maximize a policy-gradient surrogate subject to a hard average-KL trust-region constraint (δ = 0.01), solved via conjugate gradient and a line search with an analytically-computed Fisher information matrix. Solves MuJoCo locomotion (swimmer/hopper/walker) from scratch where natural gradient and derivative-free methods fail, and produces competitive Atari policies from raw pixels — the direct theoretical ancestor PPO's first-order clip approximates.

papers.title: The future of employment: How susceptible are jobs to computerisation?
papers.abstract: Trains a Gaussian process classifier on 70 hand-labelled O*NET occupations, scored across nine perception/manipulation, creative-intelligence and social-intelligence variables, to estimate a probability of computerisation for 702 US occupations. Finds 47% of US employment in a high-risk category with a strong negative wage/education gradient — the canonical occupation-level automation-exposure baseline that OECD's task-level re-estimate (~9%) and later LLM-exposure work (GPTs are GPTs) directly react against.

papers.title: Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning
papers.authors: Moritz Hardt, Eric Price, Nathan Srebro
papers.abstract: Introduces equalized odds and its relaxation equal opportunity — oblivious fairness criteria requiring a predictor's true/false-positive rates to match across a protected group — plus an efficient post-processing method that converts any trained score into a compliant, provably near-Bayes-optimal classifier without retraining. A FICO-credit-score case study shows equal opportunity costs under a quarter of what demographic parity costs in forgone profit, while a companion impossibility result shows no oblivious audit (including this one) can fully certify a predictor as non-discriminatory.

papers.title: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs
papers.abstract: Introduces 4-bit NormalFloat quantization, Double Quantization, and Paged Optimizers so gradients can backpropagate through a frozen 4-bit base model into 16-bit LoRA adapters, cutting 65B-parameter finetuning memory from over 780GB to under 48GB with no measured loss versus full 16-bit finetuning. The resulting Guanaco chatbot family reaches 99.3% of ChatGPT's score on the GPT-4-judged Vicuna benchmark after 24 hours of finetuning on a single GPU.

papers.title: Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions and Perturbations
papers.abstract: Introduces IMAGENET-C (15 algorithmic corruptions × 5 severities) and IMAGENET-P (temporal perturbation sequences) as standardized benchmarks for common, non-adversarial robustness. Absolute corruption error (mCE) improves with architecture progress from AlexNet to ResNet-50, but Relative mCE — which isolates corruption damage from each model's own clean-accuracy baseline — does not, meaning a decade of architectural gains bought almost no dedicated corruption robustness.

papers.title: Human-level control through deep reinforcement learning
papers.authors: Mnih, Kavukcuoglu, Silver, Rusu, Veness, Bellemare, Graves, Riedmiller, Fidjeland, Ostrovski, Petersen, Beattie, Sadik, Antonoglou, King, Kumaran, Wierstra, Legg, Hassabis
papers.abstract: Trains a single convolutional network end-to-end from raw Atari 2600 pixels and the game score alone, stabilized by two additions to Q-learning — a replay buffer that decorrelates updates and a periodically-frozen target network — to reach a broadly human-comparable level across 49 different games using one fixed architecture and hyperparameter set. Beats the best prior hand-engineered-feature RL methods on 43/49 games and clears 75% of human performance on 29/49, while scoring 0 on long-horizon-exploration games like Montezuma's Revenge.

papers.title: MuJoCo: A physics engine for model-based control
papers.abstract: Introduces MuJoCo, a physics simulator purpose-built for model-based control rather than graphics: articulated systems in generalized coordinates paired with a convex, soft contact/constraint model that stays fast, numerically stable, and — unusually — invertible even with contact. It went on to become the default simulator underlying a decade of deep-RL continuous-control research, from TRPO and PPO to Soft Actor-Critic and TD3.

papers.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning with Double Q-learning
papers.abstract: Proves that Q-learning's max operator produces a provable, unavoidable overestimation bias whenever value estimates carry any error, and shows this bias is real and harmful even in the near-ideal, deterministic Atari setting that made DQN famous. Double DQN fixes it almost for free by reusing DQN's existing target network to evaluate the action the online network selects — no new parameters — lifting median human-starts scores across 49 games from 47.5% (DQN) to 116.7% (tuned Double DQN).

papers.title: Continuous Control with Deep Reinforcement Learning
papers.authors: Lillicrap, Hunt, Pritzel, Heess, Erez, Tassa, Silver, Wierstra
papers.abstract: Adapts DQN's off-policy stabilizers — a replay buffer and target networks, plus batch normalization — to a deterministic actor-critic built on the DPG theorem, letting one shared architecture and hyperparameter set robustly solve more than 20 continuous-action MuJoCo/TORCS physics tasks, in many cases learning end-to-end from raw pixels within 2.5M environment steps.

papers.title: GPT-4o System Card
papers.authors: OpenAI (Hurst, Lerer, Goucher, Perelman, Ramesh, et al.)
papers.abstract: OpenAI's safety and capability report on GPT-4o, its first end-to-end omni model spanning text, vision, and audio: a Preparedness Framework scorecard (Low risk in cybersecurity, biological threats, and model autonomy; borderline-Medium in persuasion, setting the overall risk to Medium), four phases of external red-teaming across 100+ testers in 45 languages, and third-party assessments from METR and Apollo Research — though neither independent assessment covered the model's voice-specific safety claims.

papers.title: Making the V in VQA Matter: Elevating the Role of Image Understanding in Visual Question Answering
papers.abstract: Balances the original VQA dataset by pairing every question with two visually similar images that have two different answers, growing it to ~1.1M (image, question) pairs and ~13M answers over the same ~200K COCO images. Every 2016 state-of-the-art VQA model scores meaningfully worse on the balanced set than the original, confirming they had been exploiting language priors rather than reading the image.

papers.title: Soft Actor-Critic: Off-Policy Maximum Entropy Deep Reinforcement Learning with a Stochastic Actor
papers.abstract: Adds an entropy bonus directly into the RL objective, giving an off-policy actor-critic algorithm that trains a reparameterized stochastic Gaussian policy with a clipped double-Q critic (borrowed from concurrent TD3 work). Outperforms DDPG and PPO by a large margin on hard MuJoCo continuous-control tasks including the 21-dimensional Humanoid, though reward scale remains a per-task hyperparameter — automatic temperature tuning arrived only in a later follow-up paper.

papers.title: Addressing Function Approximation Error in Actor-Critic Methods
papers.abstract: Proves that DDPG's deterministic policy gradient suffers the same overestimation bias that plagues Q-learning, then fixes it with three additions: a clipped twin critic (taking the minimum of two Q-estimates), delayed actor/target updates, and target policy smoothing. Across 10 seeds on OpenAI Gym's MuJoCo suite the resulting TD3 algorithm roughly doubles DDPG's return on HalfCheetah, Walker2d, and Ant, and — alongside SAC — became the field's default continuous-control baseline.

papers.title: OpenAI Gym
papers.authors: Brockman, Cheung, Pettersson, Schneider, Schulman, Tang, Zaremba
papers.abstract: A common environment interface (reset/step) plus a growing benchmark suite spanning classic control, Atari, board games, algorithmic tasks, and MuJoCo-wrapped continuous control, meant to make reinforcement-learning results comparable across labs rather than just make environment code reusable.

papers.title: TriviaQA: A Large Scale Distantly Supervised Challenge Dataset for Reading Comprehension
papers.abstract: A reading-comprehension dataset built backwards from the usual recipe: 95,956 question-answer pairs authored independently by trivia enthusiasts, then paired after the fact with 650K+ evidence documents retrieved via Wikipedia and Bing web search. The best 2017 baseline reaches only 40% exact match against 79.7% human accuracy, but the distant-supervision pipeline is itself noisy — only 75-80% of automatically retrieved evidence documents actually support their answer. Today it functions less as an RC frontier and more as a fast closed-book factual-recall check baked into evals for GPT-3, SimpleQA, and BrowseComp.

papers.title: Qwen Technical Report
papers.authors: Bai, Bai, Chu, et al. (Qwen Team, Alibaba Group)
papers.abstract: Alibaba's first open LLM family: 1.8B/7B/14B base models pretrained on up to 3 trillion tokens with a custom 152K-vocabulary tokenizer, aligned into Qwen-Chat via SFT and PPO-based RLHF. Qwen-14B beats the 5x-larger LLaMA 2-70B on C-Eval, MATH, and HumanEval, and Qwen-Chat-14B outperforms GPT-4 on an in-house ReAct tool-selection benchmark (98% vs 95% accuracy) — though that agent-eval result is Qwen-authored and Qwen-graded, not independently verified.

papers.title: Teaching Machines to Read and Comprehend
papers.authors: Hermann, Kočiský, Grefenstette, Espeholt, Kay, Suleyman, Blunsom
papers.abstract: A cloze-style reading comprehension corpus built automatically from roughly 1M CNN and Daily Mail news articles paired with their bullet-point summaries, with entities anonymized via coreference resolution and randomly re-permuted per example so a model must ground every answer in the document rather than world knowledge. The Attentive and Impatient Reader — early neural attention architectures introduced alongside it — reach 63.0%/69.0% and 63.8%/68.0% CNN/Daily Mail test accuracy, beating a frame-semantic-parsing baseline (40.2%/35.5%) by 23-33 points.

papers.title: Robots and Jobs: Evidence from US Labor Markets
papers.abstract: Instruments US commuting-zone exposure to industrial robots with robot-adoption trends in five more-automated European countries; one more robot per thousand workers cuts local employment by 0.39 percentage points and wages by 0.77% (2SLS), with a model-calibrated aggregate effect of 0.2 points / 0.42% (~400,000 jobs, 1990–2007). The landmark real-world empirical counterpart to classifier-based automation-risk estimates like Frey & Osborne's.

papers.title: A Survey on Evaluation of Large Language Models
papers.abstract: The field's first systematic taxonomy of LLM evaluation, organized around what to evaluate, where (46 benchmarks), and how (metrics and protocols) — synthesized from 269 papers, plus a summary of success/failure patterns and seven open grand challenges.

papers.title: Fairness Through Awareness
papers.authors: Cynthia Dwork, Moritz Hardt, Toniann Pitassi, Omer Reingold, Richard Zemel
papers.abstract: Introduces individual fairness: a randomized classifier is fair if it satisfies a Lipschitz condition D(M(x),M(y)) ≤ d(x,y) with respect to a task-specific similarity metric on individuals, so similar people receive similar outcome distributions. Shows statistical (group) parity is inadequate via three worked counterexamples, gives an exact Earthmover-distance characterization of when the Lipschitz condition implies parity, and connects the framework to differential privacy.

papers.title: The Arcade Learning Environment: An Evaluation Platform for General Agents
papers.abstract: Wraps the open-source Stella emulator in a single interface exposing hundreds of independently-authored Atari 2600 games as pixel or RAM observations with 18 discrete actions, and proposes a disjoint 5-game train / 50-game test evaluation methodology plus three score-normalization schemes — the benchmark platform the entire pre-LLM deep-RL era (DQN, A3C, Rainbow) evaluated on.

papers.title: Semantics derived automatically from language corpora contain human-like biases
papers.abstract: Introduces WEAT, a permutation-test analogue of the psychologist's Implicit Association Test applied to word-vector cosine similarities, and shows that off-the-shelf GloVe embeddings trained on ordinary web text replicate a spectrum of documented human biases — flowers/insects, race, gender-career, and gender-science associations — at effect sizes comparable to the IAT literature they replicate. A companion test, WEFAT, shows the same embeddings also encode veridical real-world regularities, like the actual gender split of occupations (ρ = 0.90).

papers.title: Datasheets for Datasets
papers.authors: Gebru, Morgenstern, Vecchione, Vaughan, Wallach, Daumé III, Crawford
papers.abstract: Proposes a standard questionnaire — motivation, composition, collection process, preprocessing, uses, distribution, maintenance — for documenting a dataset before release, modeled on the electronics industry's component datasheet. Refined over ~2 years via pilots at Microsoft/Google/IBM and public arXiv feedback; seeded Model Cards, FactSheets, and the Dataset Nutrition Label.

papers.title: Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction
papers.author_group: University of Massachusetts Amherst / University of Alberta
papers.abstract: The foundational RL textbook unifying dynamic programming, Monte Carlo methods, and temporal-difference learning under one MDP/value-function/Bellman-equation framework — the shared theoretical ancestor nearly every RL paper in this graph (DQN, A3C, TRPO, DDPG, SAC, TD3) directly cites.

papers.title: Don’t Stop Pretraining: Adapt Language Models to Domains and Tasks
papers.authors: Gururangan, Marasović, Swayamdipta, Lo, Beltagy, Downey, Smith
papers.abstract: Continuing RoBERTa's pretraining on domain-specific unlabeled text (DAPT) improves 7 of 8 tasks across four domains (BioMed, CS, News, Reviews), and adapting further to the task's own unlabeled text (TAPT) helps again on top of it; a small pool of human-curated task data, or a cheap automatic nearest-neighbor selection from the domain corpus, closes most of the remaining gap at a fraction of DAPT's cost.

papers.title: Microsoft COCO Captions: Data Collection and Evaluation Server
papers.authors: Chen, Fang, Lin, Vedantam, Gupta, Dollár, Zitnick
papers.abstract: Introduces the MS COCO Caption dataset — five independent human captions per image across COCO's training, validation, and test splits — alongside a CodaLab-hosted evaluation server that scores submissions with BLEU, ROUGE-L, METEOR, and CIDEr-D while keeping test-set references private. Reports human-vs-human agreement under each metric (CIDEr-D highest at 0.854) as the honest ceiling any captioning system can be judged against; the resulting dataset + server became the field's standard captioning benchmark for years.

papers.title: CLIPScore: A Reference-free Evaluation Metric for Image Captioning
papers.authors: Hessel, Holtzman, Forbes, Le Bras, Choi
papers.author_group: Allen Institute for AI / University of Washington
papers.abstract: Shows that a frozen, off-the-shelf CLIP's image-text cosine similarity — with no reference captions at all — correlates with human caption-quality judgment better than reference-based metrics like CIDEr and SPICE, and introduces RefCLIPScore to blend in references when available.

papers.title: ImageNet-trained CNNs are biased towards texture; increasing shape bias improves accuracy and robustness
papers.authors: Robert Geirhos, Patricia Rubisch, Claudio Michaelis, Matthias Bethge, Felix A. Wichmann, Wieland Brendel
papers.abstract: A rigorous psychophysics study (97 lab observers, 48,560 trials) generates texture-shape cue-conflict images via neural style transfer and finds humans resolve them by voting shape 95.9% of the time while four standard ImageNet-trained CNNs vote texture 57-83% of the time. Retraining the identical ResNet-50 on Stylized-ImageNet lifts its shape bias from 22% to 81% and, as a byproduct, improves classification accuracy, object-detection transfer, and corruption robustness (ImageNet-C mCE 76.7 to 69.3).

papers.title: Unbiased Look at Dataset Bias
papers.abstract: A 12-way linear classifier can guess which of twelve popular computer-vision datasets a photo came from with 39% accuracy (chance is 8%), and object detectors trained on one dataset lose up to 73% of their accuracy when tested on another — Torralba and Efros name and measure this gap as "dataset bias."

papers.title: Simple Statistical Gradient-Following Algorithms for Connectionist Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: Defines the REINFORCE class of algorithms and proves that any weight update of the form (reward − baseline) × a unit's own characteristic eligibility is, in expectation, an unbiased ascent step on the gradient of expected reward, without ever explicitly forming a gradient estimate. Lays the mathematical foundation — the score-function/likelihood-ratio gradient — that actor-critic, TRPO, PPO, A3C, and modern RLHF/GRPO all build on.

papers.title: Model Cards for Model Reporting
papers.authors: Mitchell, Wu, Zaldivar, Barnes, Vasserman, Hutchinson, Spitzer, Raji, Gebru
papers.abstract: Proposes model cards — short standardized documents accompanying a released trained model that disclose intended use, factors, metrics, and quantitative analyses disaggregated across demographic/phenotypic/environmental subgroups and their intersections. Demonstrated on a CelebA smiling classifier and Perspective API's TOXICITY text classifier.

papers.title: Fair Prediction with Disparate Impact: A Study of Bias in Recidivism Prediction Instruments
papers.abstract: Proves that calibration (equal predictive value across groups) and equalized false-positive/false-negative rates are generically incompatible whenever recidivism prevalence differs by group, explaining why ProPublica's and Northpointe's competing claims about the COMPAS instrument were both technically correct. Grounded in Broward County COMPAS data: Black defendants (51% recidivism prevalence) face a 45% false-positive rate vs. 23% for White defendants (39% prevalence).

papers.title: Understanding Contrastive Representation Learning through Alignment and Uniformity on the Hypersphere
papers.abstract: Proposes two computable, differentiable metrics for representation quality — alignment (mean distance between positive-pair embeddings) and uniformity (how evenly embeddings spread across the unit hypersphere) — proves the contrastive loss optimizes both asymptotically, and shows that directly optimizing them alone matches or beats standard contrastive learning on STL-10, NYU-Depth-V2, and ImageNet.

papers.title: SPICE: Semantic Propositional Image Caption Evaluation
papers.abstract: Proposes SPICE, an automatic caption-evaluation metric that parses candidate and reference captions into scene graphs and scores an F1 over matched object/attribute/relation tuples rather than n-gram overlap. Reaches a 0.88 Pearson correlation with human judgment on the 2015 COCO Captioning Challenge (versus 0.43 for CIDEr and 0.53 for METEOR).

papers.title: GPTQ: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for Generative Pre-trained Transformers
papers.abstract: A one-shot, layer-wise weight-quantization method based on approximate second-order (Hessian) information, quantizing GPT-scale models up to 175 billion parameters to 3-4 bits per weight in about four GPU-hours on a single A100, losing only 0.03 WikiText2 perplexity points at 4-bit on OPT-175B, and delivering measured 3.24x-4.53x inference-latency speedups.

papers.title: Deep Learning Face Attributes in the Wild
papers.author_group: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
papers.abstract: Introduces CelebA — 200,000 celebrity face images (202,599 images / 10,177 identities in its public release), each annotated with 40 binary attributes and 5 landmarks — alongside a two-stage LNet+ANet CNN cascade that localizes faces from image-level attribute tags alone. Became the field's default face-attribute, face-verification, and face-generation benchmark for the following decade.

papers.title: Qwen2 Technical Report
papers.authors: Qwen Team (An Yang, Baosong Yang, et al.)
papers.abstract: Alibaba's second open LLM generation: five models (0.5B–72B dense, plus a 57B-A14B Mixture-of-Experts) pre-trained on over 7 trillion tokens and extended to 128K-token context, then aligned with SFT and a two-stage offline+online DPO pipeline. Qwen2-72B-Instruct beats Llama-3-70B-Instruct on MMLU-Pro, HumanEval, and Arena-Hard — while a self-reported contamination table flags up to 75.0% of HumanEval test samples as overlapping the training data under a strict 13-gram rule.

papers.title: MMBench: Is Your Multi-modal Model an All-around Player?
papers.authors: Liu, Duan, Zhang, Li, Zhang, Zhao, Yuan, Wang, He, Liu, Chen, Lin
papers.abstract: A bilingual, 3,217-question multiple-choice benchmark spanning a hierarchical 20-leaf ability taxonomy (Perception/Reasoning), evaluated with a CircularEval protocol that requires a model to answer correctly across every circularly-shifted ordering of a question's choices, plus GPT-4-assisted extraction of free-form answers into labeled choices.

papers.title: ColBERT: Efficient and Effective Passage Search via Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT
papers.abstract: Introduces late interaction: encode queries and documents independently with BERT into per-token embeddings, then score with a cheap MaxSim operator, letting document representations be pre-computed offline. Reaches 34.9 MRR@10 on MS MARCO (matching BERT-base's 34.7) at over 170× lower re-ranking latency and 13,900× fewer FLOPs/query.

papers.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning that Matters
papers.authors: Henderson, Islam, Bachman, Pineau, Precup, Meger
papers.abstract: A systematic reproducibility audit of TRPO, DDPG, PPO, and ACKTR on MuJoCo continuous-control tasks, showing that random seed, network architecture, reward scale, and even which codebase implements 'the same' algorithm can each move reported results as much as the claimed algorithmic gains. Proposes significance testing and full disclosure of hyperparameters/codebases as partial fixes.

papers.title: SemEval-2017 Task 1: Semantic Textual Similarity Multilingual and Crosslingual Focused Evaluation
papers.abstract: Introduces the SemEval-2017 multilingual/cross-lingual STS shared task and releases the STS Benchmark — 8,628 English sentence pairs (5,749/1,500/1,379 train/dev/test) that became the standard STS-B target for the sentence-embedding literature.

papers.title: Labeled Faces in the Wild: A Database for Studying Face Recognition in Unconstrained Environments
papers.abstract: Introduces LFW — 13,233 face images of 5,749 individuals (1,680 with two or more photos) collected from news photographs and detected via Viola-Jones — and the 'unseen pair matching' verification protocol (View 1 for model selection; View 2's 10-fold leave-one-out split for performance reporting) that became face recognition's default unconstrained benchmark for the next decade.

papers.title: Inherent Trade-Offs in the Fair Determination of Risk Scores
papers.authors: Jon Kleinberg, Sendhil Mullainathan, Manish Raghavan
papers.abstract: Formalizes three fairness conditions for risk assignments — calibration within groups, balance for the negative class, and balance for the positive class — and proves they cannot all hold simultaneously except when the compared groups share a base rate or the predictor achieves perfect prediction, with the impossibility surviving in an ε-approximate form.

papers.title: Automatic Evaluation of Summaries Using N-gram Co-occurrence Statistics
papers.abstract: Ports BLEU's n-gram co-occurrence scoring from machine translation to automatic summary evaluation, reframing it as a recall-based metric to match DUC's coverage-based human judging protocol. On DUC 2001 data, plain unigram co-occurrence correlates far more consistently with human rankings (Spearman ρ up to 0.99) than BLEU-style weighted multi-length matching — the recall framing Lin's own ROUGE would generalize a year later.

papers.title: Calibrate Before Use: Improving Few-Shot Performance of Language Models
papers.abstract: Shows that GPT-3's few-shot accuracy is highly unstable across choices of prompt format, training-example selection, and order — the same examples, merely reordered, can swing accuracy from near-chance to near-state-of-the-art — and traces this to majority-label, recency, and common-token biases. Introduces contextual calibration, a training-free correction using a content-free input (e.g. "N/A") to estimate and remove the bias, improving accuracy by up to 30.0% absolute.

papers.title: Spider: A Large-Scale Human-Labeled Dataset for Complex and Cross-Domain Semantic Parsing and Text-to-SQL Task
papers.authors: Yu, Zhang, Yang, Yasunaga, Wang, et al.
papers.abstract: The canonical cross-domain text-to-SQL benchmark: 10,181 questions and 5,693 unique complex SQL queries across 200 multi-table databases spanning 138 domains, split so no database's schema appears in both training and test. The best 2018 baseline (SQLNet) reaches only 12.4% exact-match accuracy under this database split, exposing how badly prior single-database, template-memorizing evaluations had inflated the field's sense of progress.

papers.title: Automatic Evaluation of Machine Translation Quality Using N-gram Co-occurrence Statistics
papers.abstract: Reworks IBM's BLEU into the NIST MT metric: replaces BLEU's geometric mean over n-gram orders with an arithmetic sum, weights each n-gram by an information value (how unpredictable it is given its prefix) so rare, meaning-bearing n-grams count more than common filler, and recalibrates the brevity penalty. Beats BLEU's Adequacy correlation on all four DARPA corpora tested, though the paper's own ablation shows information weighting clearly helping only at unigrams.

papers.title: On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks
papers.abstract: Shows that modern deep classifiers (ResNets, DenseNets, Wide ResNets) are far more confidently wrong than older, shallower networks despite being far more accurate, and traces the miscalibration to model capacity, Batch Normalization, and vanishing weight decay. Introduces temperature scaling, a single-parameter softmax rescaling that beats histogram binning, isotonic regression, and Platt-scaling variants at fixing it.

papers.title: A Study of Translation Edit Rate with Targeted Human Annotation
papers.abstract: TER scores machine-translation output by the minimum number of edits — insertions, deletions, substitutions, and unit-cost block shifts — needed to reach a reference, normalized by reference length. Its human-in-the-loop variant, HTER, correlates with average human judgment (r=0.630) better than two independent human judges agree with each other (r=0.478).

papers.title: Exploiting Cloze-Questions for Few-Shot Text Classification and Natural Language Inference
papers.abstract: Introduces Pattern-Exploiting Training (PET): reformulate each task as a cloze-style question via hand-written pattern-verbalizer pairs, finetune a separate small model per pattern on a handful of labeled examples, then distill the ensemble's soft labels on a large pool of unlabeled data into one classifier. With just 10 labeled examples, PET lifts RoBERTa-large's accuracy from near-chance to 87.5% on AG's News — an influential pre-FLAN, pre-GPT-3 prompt-based finetuning recipe.

papers.title: Leveraging Passage Retrieval with Generative Models for Open Domain Question Answering
papers.abstract: Feeds passages retrieved by BM25 or DPR into a sequence-to-sequence reader that encodes each passage independently and fuses them only in the decoder's cross-attention, so accuracy keeps rising as more passages are retrieved. Sets new state-of-the-art exact-match results on NaturalQuestions and TriviaQA with far fewer parameters than closed-book generative QA models.

papers.title: Rainbow: Combining Improvements in Deep Reinforcement Learning
papers.authors: Hessel, Modayil, Van Hasselt, Schaul, Ostrovski, Dabney, Horgan, Piot, Azar, Silver
papers.abstract: Combines six independently-published DQN extensions — Double Q-learning, prioritized replay, dueling networks, multi-step returns, distributional RL (C51), and Noisy Nets — into a single agent that reaches 223% median human-normalized score across 57 Atari games. A detailed ablation study finds prioritized replay and multi-step learning matter most, with dueling and double Q-learning barely moving the aggregate median.

papers.title: Deterministic Policy Gradient Algorithms
papers.authors: Silver, Lever, Heess, Degris, Wierstra, Riedmiller
papers.abstract: Proves the deterministic policy gradient theorem — the gradient of expected return is just the expected gradient of the critic's action-value through the policy's own parameters, no integral over actions — and builds an off-policy actor-critic (COPDAC) on top of it that beats stochastic policy gradients by orders of magnitude in high-dimensional continuous control.
